**Bill Plympton** (born April 30, 1946)[[1]](#cite_note-filmfestival-1) is an American [animator](/wiki/Animation "Animation"), [graphic designer](/wiki/Graphic_designer "Graphic designer"), [cartoonist](/wiki/Cartoonist "Cartoonist"), and filmmaker best known for his 1987 [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award")–nominated [animated short](/wiki/Animated_short "Animated short") *[Your Face](/wiki/Your_Face_(film) "Your Face (film)")* and his series of shorts featuring a dog character starting with 2004's *[Guard Dog](/wiki/Guard_Dog_(film) "Guard Dog (film)")*.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bill_Plympton&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Plympton was born in [Portland, Oregon](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon"), the son of Wilda Jean (Jerman) and Donald F. Plympton,[[3]](#cite_note-plymptonbio-3)[[4]](#cite_note-wilda-4) and was raised on a farm in nearby [Oregon City](/wiki/Oregon_City,_Oregon "Oregon City, Oregon")[[1]](#cite_note-filmfestival-1) with five siblings.[[4]](#cite_note-wilda-4) From 1964 to 1968, he studied [Graphic Design](/wiki/Graphic_Design "Graphic Design") at [Portland State University](/wiki/Portland_State_University "Portland State University"), where he was a member of the film society and worked on the [yearbook](/wiki/Yearbook "Yearbook"). In 1968, he transferred to the [School of Visual Arts](/wiki/School_of_Visual_Arts "School of Visual Arts")[[3]](#cite_note-plymptonbio-3) in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), where he majored in [cartooning](/wiki/Cartooning "Cartooning"). He graduated from SVA in 1969.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bill_Plympton&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

Plympton's illustrations and cartoons have been published in *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* and the weekly newspaper *[The Village Voice](/wiki/The_Village_Voice "The Village Voice")*, as well as in the magazines *[Vogue](/wiki/Vogue_magazine "Vogue magazine")*, *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")*, *[Vanity Fair](/wiki/Vanity_Fair_(magazine) "Vanity Fair (magazine)")*, *[Penthouse](/wiki/Penthouse_magazine "Penthouse magazine")*, and *[National Lampoon](/wiki/National_Lampoon_(magazine) "National Lampoon (magazine)")*. His [political cartoon](/wiki/Political_cartoon "Political cartoon") strip *Plympton*, which began in 1975 in the *[SoHo Weekly News](/wiki/SoHo_Weekly_News "SoHo Weekly News")*, eventually was [syndicated](/wiki/Print_syndication "Print syndication") and appeared in over 20 newspapers.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In 1988, his [animated short](/wiki/Animated_short "Animated short") *[Your Face](/wiki/Your_Face_(film) "Your Face (film)")* was nominated for the [Academy Award for Best Animated Short Film](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Animated_Short_Film "Academy Award for Best Animated Short Film"). He also became known for other animated short films, including *25 Ways to Quit Smoking* (1989) and *Enemies* (1991), the latter of which was part of the *Animania* series on [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV"), where many of his other shorts were shown.[[7]](#cite_note-Tune-7)

In 1991, Plympton won the [Prix Spécial du Jury](/wiki/Short_Film_Palme_d%27Or "Short Film Palme d'Or") at the [Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/Cannes_Film_Festival "Cannes Film Festival") for *Push Comes to Shove* which was featured on MTV's animated series *[Liquid Television](/wiki/Liquid_Television "Liquid Television")*.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In 1992, his self-financed, first feature-length animated film, *[The Tune](/wiki/The_Tune "The Tune")* debuted at the [Sundance Film Festival](/wiki/Sundance_Film_Festival "Sundance Film Festival").[[7]](#cite_note-Tune-7)
His work also appeared on the 1992–1993 [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company") comedy series *[The Edge](/wiki/The_Edge_(TV_series) "The Edge (TV series)")*. In 1993, he made his first live action film, *J. Lyle*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Bill_and_Sandrine_at_IADT.jpg/250px-Bill_and_Sandrine_at_IADT.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bill_and_Sandrine_at_IADT.jpg)

Bill and Sandrine Plympton in 2014

In 1995, he contributed animation and graphics to a [computer game](/wiki/Computer_game "Computer game") collection, *[Take Your Best Shot](/wiki/Take_Your_Best_Shot_(video_game) "Take Your Best Shot (video game)")*.[[9]](#cite_note-9) He also published a [comic book](/wiki/Comic_book "Comic book") in 2003, *The Sleazy Cartoons of Bill Plympton*.

The actress [Martha Plimpton](/wiki/Martha_Plimpton "Martha Plimpton"), a distant relative of his,[[10]](#cite_note-10) served as associate producer on Plympton's animated feature *[Hair High](/wiki/Hair_High "Hair High")* (2004), doing much of the casting. The movie's voice cast included her father [Keith Carradine](/wiki/Keith_Carradine "Keith Carradine") and her uncle [David Carradine](/wiki/David_Carradine "David Carradine").

## Later works

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bill_Plympton&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Later works")]

*[Guard Dog](/wiki/Guard_Dog_(film) "Guard Dog (film)")* (2004) was also nominated for the Academy Award for Best Animated Short Film. In 2005, Plympton animated a music video for [Kanye West](/wiki/Kanye_West "Kanye West")'s "[Heard 'Em Say](/wiki/Heard_%27Em_Say "Heard 'Em Say")" and the following year, he created the music video for ["Weird Al" Yankovic](/wiki/%22Weird_Al%22_Yankovic "\"Weird Al\" Yankovic")'s "[Don't Download This Song](/wiki/Don%27t_Download_This_Song "Don't Download This Song")". Plympton contributed animation to the 2006 [History Channel](/wiki/History_(U.S._TV_channel) "History (U.S. TV channel)") series *[10 Days That Unexpectedly Changed America](/wiki/10_Days_That_Unexpectedly_Changed_America "10 Days That Unexpectedly Changed America")*, to illustrate the events of [Shays' Rebellion](/wiki/Shays%27_Rebellion "Shays' Rebellion"). Together with other independent New York City animators, he has released two [DVDs](/wiki/DVD "DVD") of [animated shorts](/wiki/Animated_short "Animated short"), both titled *Avoid Eye Contact*.

Plympton's 2008 80-minute feature, *Idiots and Angels* presented by [Terry Gilliam](/wiki/Terry_Gilliam "Terry Gilliam"), had no dialogue.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) The film premiered at the [Tribeca Film Festival](/wiki/Tribeca_Film_Festival "Tribeca Film Festival") on 26 April 2008,[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In 2011, Alexia Anastasio completed a documentary on Plympton's life, *Adventures in Plymptoons!*,[[14]](#cite_note-14) released in September 2012 [direct-to-DVD](/wiki/Direct-to-DVD "Direct-to-DVD") and on [video-on-demand](/wiki/Video-on-demand "Video-on-demand").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In 2011, Plympton collaborated with child film critic Perry Chen on *Ingrid Pitt: Beyond the Forest*, a 2011 short animated film directed by [Kevin Sean Michaels](/wiki/Kevin_Sean_Michaels "Kevin Sean Michaels"), about actress and [Holocaust](/wiki/Holocaust "Holocaust") survivor [Ingrid Pitt](/wiki/Ingrid_Pitt "Ingrid Pitt").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Plympton has done extensive guest animation work for *[The Simpsons](/wiki/The_Simpsons "The Simpsons")*, producing eight opening [couch gags](/wiki/Couch_gag "Couch gag") for the show between 2012 and 2022, as well as the menus and packaging for the Season 19 DVD.

Plympton directed the segment "On Eating and Drinking" in the 2014 animated film *[The Prophet](/wiki/The_Prophet_(2014_film) "The Prophet (2014 film)")*, adapted from [Kahlil Gibran](/wiki/Kahlil_Gibran "Kahlil Gibran")'s book *[The Prophet.](/wiki/The_Prophet_(book) "The Prophet (book)")* In 2018, Plympton created a series of videos for *The New York Times* called “Trump Bites”. One of the series, *Trump and Putin: A Love Story*, depicts Trump and Putin kissing half-naked. Critics said the video implied that gay relationships were inherently comical and immoral.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In 2020, Plympton released a [Kickstarter](/wiki/Kickstarter "Kickstarter") for a new animated comedy western, *Slide*. The funding was successful, raising $60,000 from 400 backers. The film premiered at the 2023 [Annecy International Animation Film Festival](/wiki/Annecy_International_Animation_Film_Festival "Annecy International Animation Film Festival") and was shown at several other film festivals through 2023 and 2024. In November 2025, *Slide* was released to 80 screens in France with the title *Duel à Monte-Carlo del Norte*.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In August 2025, Plympton announced a new animated short entitled *Whale 52 - Suite for Man, Boy, and Whale* which had its New York premiere in September at the [IFC Center](/wiki/IFC_Center "IFC Center") in New York City as part of a multi-day Plympton retrospective produced by ["Weird Al" Yankovic](/wiki/%22Weird_Al%22_Yankovic "\"Weird Al\" Yankovic"), where another Plympton animated short, *Duckville*, also had its NYC premiere. *Whale 52* played at the 2025 [Woodstock Film Festival](/wiki/Woodstock_Film_Festival "Woodstock Film Festival") and was one of 113 Oscar-qualified animated shorts.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) Although it was not nominated for an Academy Award, in February 2026 *Whale 52* screened at the [Berlin Film Festival](/wiki/Berlin_International_Film_Festival "Berlin International Film Festival") and received the Crystal Bear award for Best Short Film.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Technique

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bill_Plympton&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Technique")]

By sometimes alternating the same drawings back and forth, it creates a shimmering effect, known in animation as a 'boil', that makes it look like the images are pulsating.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bill_Plympton&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Legacy")]

A collection of more than 180 Plympton items is held at the [Academy Film Archive](/wiki/Academy_Film_Archive "Academy Film Archive").[[27]](#cite_note-27) The archive has preserved Plympton's films such as *Your Face*, *The Tune*, *Guard Dog*, and *The Cow Who Wanted to Be a Hamburger*.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

His films have featured in the [Animation Show of Shows](/wiki/Animation_show_of_shows "Animation show of shows") including *[Your Face](/wiki/Your_Face_(film) "Your Face (film)")*, *Guard Dog*, *Eat* (2001), *The Fan and the Flower* (2005), and *Santa: The Fascist Years* (2009).[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bill_Plympton&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Personal life")]

On December 23, 2011, Plympton married animator/artist/illustrator Sandrine Flament.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35) Their son was born in September 2012.[[1]](#cite_note-filmfestival-1)

## Influences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bill_Plympton&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Influences")]

Plympton has stated he has many influences, the biggest being the work of the [Walt Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney "Walt Disney") with others including [Tex Avery](/wiki/Tex_Avery "Tex Avery"), [Bob Clampett](/wiki/Bob_Clampett "Bob Clampett"), [Robert Crumb](/wiki/Robert_Crumb "Robert Crumb"), [Milton Glaser](/wiki/Milton_Glaser "Milton Glaser"), [Charles Addams](/wiki/Charles_Addams "Charles Addams"), [Charles M. Schulz](/wiki/Charles_M._Schulz "Charles M. Schulz"), [Frank Capra](/wiki/Frank_Capra "Frank Capra"), [Bob Godfrey](/wiki/Bob_Godfrey "Bob Godfrey"), [Tomi Ungerer](/wiki/Tomi_Ungerer "Tomi Ungerer"), [Quentin Tarantino](/wiki/Quentin_Tarantino "Quentin Tarantino"), [Jacques Tati](/wiki/Jacques_Tati "Jacques Tati"), [Don Martin](/wiki/Don_Martin_(cartoonist) "Don Martin (cartoonist)"), [Harvey Kurtzman](/wiki/Harvey_Kurtzman "Harvey Kurtzman"), [Spain Rodriguez](/wiki/Spain_Rodriguez "Spain Rodriguez") and [Jules Feiffer](/wiki/Jules_Feiffer "Jules Feiffer").[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) He said *[I Married a Strange Person!](/wiki/I_Married_a_Strange_Person! "I Married a Strange Person!")* "was influenced by [Peter Jackson](/wiki/Peter_Jackson "Peter Jackson"), some of his earlier films ... where he used gore and violence and blood as humor."[[38]](#cite_note-38) He also included references to [Jacques Tati](/wiki/Jacques_Tati "Jacques Tati"),[[39]](#cite_note-39) [Carlos Nine](/wiki/Carlos_Nine "Carlos Nine"), and [Milt Kahl](/wiki/Milt_Kahl "Milt Kahl").[[40]](#cite_note-40)

## Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bill_Plympton&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Awards")]

* 1988 Academy Award nomination for Short Animation: "Your Face"[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)
* 1991 Jury Prize for Short Films, "Push Comes to Shove", [Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/Cannes_Film_Festival "Cannes Film Festival")[[43]](#cite_note-43)
* 2004 [Inkpot Award](/wiki/Inkpot_Award "Inkpot Award") winner[[44]](#cite_note-44)
* 2005 [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award") nomination for Short Animation: "Guard Dog"[[45]](#cite_note-45)
* 2006 [Winsor McCay Award](/wiki/Winsor_McCay_Award "Winsor McCay Award") [Annie Awards](/wiki/Annie_Awards "Annie Awards") by [ASIFA-Hollywood](/wiki/ASIFA-Hollywood "ASIFA-Hollywood")[[46]](#cite_note-46)
* 2011 20th Annual Cinema St. Louis Film Festival (traditional name: [St. Louis International Film Festival](/wiki/St._Louis_International_Film_Festival "St. Louis International Film Festival")), Lifetime Achievement Award[[47]](#cite_note-47)
* 2011 [Burbank International Film Festival](/wiki/Burbank_International_Film_Festival "Burbank International Film Festival"), Pioneer in Theatrical Animation Award[[48]](#cite_note-48)
* 2011 [Action On Film International Film Festival](/wiki/Action_On_Film_International_Film_Festival "Action On Film International Film Festival"), Lifetime Achievement Award[[49]](#cite_note-49)
* 2013 ANIMAFicx Award, 51st [Gijón International Film Festival](/wiki/Gij%C3%B3n_International_Film_Festival "Gijón International Film Festival"): "Cheatin'"[[50]](#cite_note-50)
* 2023 Los Angeles Animation Festival[[51]](#cite_note-51)
  + Winner Best Feature Film: "Slide"
